subject: Re: Maer Roshan
body:
please call me tomorrow late afternoon.... it's new to
me

--- Cecilia Steen <cecilia.steen@gmail.com> wrote:

> Dear Jeffrey:
> 
> For the past six weeks my lawyer has been trying to
> negotiate my severance
> agreement with MJ LLC, which was supposed to kick in
> ten days after my
> termination. My contract called for an
> eighteen-month settlement if I lost
> my job "without cause," which certainly seems
> justified given the magazine's
> premature closing. In the interest of
> expedience--and against my attorney's
> strong advice--I offered  to accept a six month
> settlement, the minimum
> provided in my contract. So I'm dismayed that this
> issue  has still not come
> to a close.
> 
> Mort's attorney says he's ready to move forward, but
> can't do so without
> your consent. Last week we were told you were too
> ill to be reached. On
> Monday, my attorney spoke with Darren Indursky who
> indicated he'd quickly
> get back to Mort's attorney with your views.
> Apparently he still hasn't
> called.
> 
> As you know, I worked incredibly hard on Radar, only
> to see the magazine
> inexplicably killed just as it was hitting its
> stride.  I lived up to my
> obligations under our agreement, so I don't
> understand why MJ is having such
> trouble meeting its own obligations, especially
> since a six month settlement
> represents a significant concession on my part.
> Since we last spoke, I've
> been in talks with Ron Burkle, who recently
> submitted a bid for the
> magazine's assets. I'm looking forward to reaching
> some closure to the past
> difficult year. But the severance issue remains a
> frustrating bit of
> unfinished business, and I'd appreciate your help in
> bringing it to a speedy
> resolution.
> 
> Thanks for your attention. I hope you're doing well.
> 
> Best,
> 
> Maer

body_html:
please call me tomorrow late afternoon.... it's new to
me

--- Cecilia Steen cecilia.steen@gmail.com wrote:

Dear Jeffrey:

For the past six weeks my lawyer has been trying to
negotiate my severance
agreement with MJ LLC, which was supposed to kick in
ten days after my
termination. My contract called for an
eighteen-month settlement if I lost
my job "without cause," which certainly seems
justified given the magazine's
premature closing. In the interest of
expedience--and against my attorney's
strong advice--I offered to accept a six month
settlement, the minimum
provided in my contract. So I'm dismayed that this
issue has still not come
to a close.

Mort's attorney says he's ready to move forward, but
can't do so without
your consent. Last week we were told you were too
ill to be reached. On
Monday, my attorney spoke with Darren Indursky who
indicated he'd quickly
get back to Mort's attorney with your views.
Apparently he still hasn't
called.

As you know, I worked incredibly hard on Radar, only
to see the magazine
inexplicably killed just as it was hitting its
stride. I lived up to my
obligations under our agreement, so I don't
understand why MJ is having such
trouble meeting its own obligations, especially
since a six month settlement
represents a significant concession on my part.
Since we last spoke, I've
been in talks with Ron Burkle, who recently
submitted a bid for the
magazine's assets. I'm looking forward to reaching
some closure to the past
difficult year. But the severance issue remains a
frustrating bit of
unfinished business, and I'd appreciate your help in
bringing it to a speedy
resolution.

Thanks for your attention. I hope you're doing well.

Best,

Maer
